skill.name: exploratory-data-analysis
skill.description: Analyze scientific data files across chemistry, genomics, microscopy, spectroscopy, proteomics, metabolomics, and general tabular formats. The skill detects file types, samples supported datasets, summarizes quality signals, and generates markdown EDA reports.
skill.summary: Analyze scientific data files and generate practical exploratory data reports.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but targeted review found most command, sensitive-file, and weak-crypto alerts are markdown or scientific-format false positives. The remaining real risk is medium: the skill includes a Python analyzer that reads arbitrary caller-provided local files, writes reports to caller-provided paths, and documents some unsafe readers for specialized formats. Publish with clear warnings to run only on trusted files in a scoped workspace.

medium_findings.title: Broad Local File Access and Report Path Disclosure
medium_findings.description: The analyzer accepts a caller-provided filepath, reads local files for analysis, records the absolute path, and writes reports to a caller-provided output path. This is expected for an EDA tool, but it can expose local path details or overwrite files if used outside a scoped workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read, absolute path reporting, and output write behavior are directly visible in the Python analyzer. The behavior appears functional rather than malicious, so the risk is contextual.

medium_findings.title: Reference Guidance Mentions Unsafe Deserialization and Subprocess Tools
medium_findings.description: Several reference files list pickle-based formats or subprocess-backed tooling as possible readers. The executable analyzer does not perform these operations, but agents following the references could use unsafe deserialization or external tools on untrusted files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky library guidance is present in documentation, but no matching execution path was found in scripts/eda_analyzer.py. This supports a medium documentation-driven risk, not a confirmed exploit.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Matches Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Many external command findings come from markdown backticks, code fences, or library examples. No subprocess, os.system, popen, exec, or eval execution path was found in the analyzer.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown examples or fenced report placeholders, not Ruby or shell execution. Targeted inspection found no executable command invocation in the Python analyzer.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive-File Matches Are Scientific Format False Positives
low_findings.description: The SAM matches refer to Sequence Alignment/Map bioinformatics files and report placeholder text, not the Windows SAM credential database. No evidence found of credential harvesting or sensitive system database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines identify the .sam scientific alignment format and generic sampling placeholders. The semantic context clearly differs from Windows credential storage.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Mostly Format-Name Collisions
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to be triggered by scientific file extensions, words such as markdown, or checksum terminology. No cryptographic implementation or authentication logic was found in the reviewed executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited material describes report text and scientific formats, not cryptographic choices. No evidence found of weak hashes used for security decisions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Caller-Controlled Output File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer writes the generated markdown report to output_path without path restrictions. This is useful, but risky in shared or privileged directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write call is direct and the output path comes from CLI arguments. Risk depends on execution privileges and workspace boundaries.

dangerous_patterns.title: Absolute Local Path Included in Generated Reports
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer stores file_path.absolute() and includes it in the markdown report. Reports can disclose local directory structure when shared.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The absolute path is explicitly captured and rendered into report text. This is a clear privacy concern, not evidence of malicious exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reference Guidance Mentions Unsafe Deserialization and Subprocess Tools
dangerous_patterns.description: Several reference files list pickle-based formats or subprocess-backed tooling as possible readers. The executable analyzer does not perform these operations, but agents following the references could use unsafe deserialization or external tools on untrusted files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky library guidance is present in documentation, but no matching execution path was found in scripts/eda_analyzer.py. This supports a medium documentation-driven risk, not a confirmed exploit.

content.value_statement: Scientific datasets often arrive in specialized formats that are hard to inspect quickly. This skill detects formats, samples supported files, and produces practical EDA reports for planning analysis.
actual_capabilities: Detects scientific file categories from extensions across several research domains.
Uses reference guides for chemistry, genomics, microscopy, spectroscopy, proteomics, and general formats.
Runs a Python analyzer for selected tabular, array, JSON, HDF5, FASTA, FASTQ, and image files.
Generates markdown reports with file metadata, type information, sampled summaries, and recommendations.
Provides EDA approaches and reader libraries for many specialized scientific file formats.
Samples large sequence and table files to reduce memory use during initial inspection.
limitations: Some formats are documented but not fully implemented in the analyzer script.
Results depend on optional Python libraries being installed in the runtime environment.
The analyzer reads local files and should be run only in a trusted workspace.
Generated reports can include local file paths and should be reviewed before sharing.

use_cases.title: Inspect New Research Files
use_cases.description: Quickly identify file type, size, structure, and likely analysis methods before deeper processing.

use_cases.title: Prepare Dataset Handoffs
use_cases.description: Create markdown summaries that explain dataset contents, quality signals, and recommended next analysis steps.

use_cases.title: Triage Multi-Format Pipelines
use_cases.description: Compare unfamiliar scientific formats and decide which Python libraries or workflows are appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Basic File Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this scientific data file and summarize its type, structure, quality signals, and recommended next steps: <file path>

prompt_templates.title: Quality-Focused EDA
prompt_templates.prompt: Run an exploratory data analysis for <file path>. Focus on missing values, dimensions, metadata, sampling limits, and quality concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Domain-Specific Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use the relevant scientific reference guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze <file path> as a <domain> dataset. Use the matching reference guide and explain suitable downstream analyses.

prompt_templates.title: Pipeline Planning Report
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for a research workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an EDA report for <file path> that includes format detection, sampled statistics, risks, required libraries, and a downstream workflow plan.

output_examples.input: A researcher asks for an EDA report on a FASTQ sequencing file.
output_examples.output: A markdown report describing read count, sampled read lengths, quality score trends, format notes, and sequencing QC recommendations.

output_examples.input: A data steward provides a CSV from an instrument export.
output_examples.output: A concise profile with row and column counts, data types, missing values, numeric summaries, and cleaning priorities.

output_examples.input: A microscopy analyst provides a TIFF image stack.
output_examples.output: A report with image dimensions, mode, value range, page count when available, and suggested imaging quality checks.

best_practices: Run the analyzer inside a scoped project directory with only the files needed for review.
Review generated reports before sharing because they may include local file paths.
Install only the domain libraries needed for the file types you plan to inspect.
anti_patterns: Do not run specialized deserializers such as pickle readers on untrusted files.
Do not point the analyzer at private system paths or unrelated directories.
Do not treat sampled EDA output as a complete validation for production analysis.

faq.question: What kinds of files can this skill inspect?
faq.answer: It covers many scientific formats and implements analysis for common tables, arrays, JSON, HDF5, sequences, and images.

faq.question: Does it require Python packages?
faq.answer: Yes. Some analyses need packages such as pandas, NumPy, h5py, Biopython, or Pillow.

faq.question: Can it analyze very large files?
faq.answer: It uses sampling for some formats, but very large files may still need domain-specific tools.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on untrusted files?
faq.answer: Use caution. Run it in a restricted workspace and avoid unsafe deserialization or external conversion tools.

faq.question: Does it send data to external services?
faq.answer: No network behavior was found in the reviewed analyzer script.

faq.question: What output does it create?
faq.answer: It creates a markdown EDA report with metadata, sampled summaries, format context, and recommended next steps.
